nativeRange.habitat: Lagoon and seaward reefs below the surge zone; slopes and drop-offs at 0–30 m depth

diet.notes: Has been reported to accept finely chopped crustacean flesh, mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, zooplankton, and frozen herbivore preparations. Generally advised to feed small amounts 2–3 times daily.

summary: A striking venomous fang blenny from the Western Pacific, generally considered reef-safe and commonly kept in established saltwater aquariums with abundant live rock. Its forked tail and yellow-blue coloration make it a popular choice for small reef tanks.
careNotes:
The Forktail Blenny is generally considered hardy and suitable for intermediate keepers, though it tends to require a mature, well-established tank with abundant live rock for grazing. It possesses small venomous canine teeth used defensively — bites from finger-feeding have been reported to be painful, so hand-feeding is best avoided. The species is a known jumper and a tight-fitting lid is strongly advised.

Best kept singly or as a mated pair; have been known to be territorial toward conspecifics and similarly shaped blennies in smaller tanks. Most sources describe them as peaceful toward unrelated species and generally compatible with corals and invertebrates.

Temperature sources disagreed notably: LiveAquaria gave 22.2–25.6°C (72–78°F), FishBase wild data gave 25.6–29.3°C, and FishKeeper.co.uk gave 23–27°C. The averaged range of 23.6–27.3°C is used here; keepers may wish to target the middle of this range (~25–26°C). Adult size also varied: FishBase and FishKeeper report 11 cm maximum TL, while LiveAquaria lists 5 inches (12.7 cm) likely reflecting a trade-size rounding; the averaged value of ~11.6 cm is used.

Males tend to be larger than females and undergo color changes during breeding. The species has been observed to spawn in captivity; males guard and fan the demersal adhesive egg clutch. Larvae have been reported to require rotifers as first food.
breedingNotes: Has been documented spawning in captivity. Males are typically larger than females and display intensified coloration during courtship. Eggs are demersal and adhesive; males have been observed guarding clutches. Larvae have been reported to require live rotifers as initial food, making captive rearing moderately challenging.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (11.0 cm TL) and temperature range (25.6–29.3°C). Temperature disagreed with LiveAquaria (22.2–25.6°C); averaged across 3 sources to derive 23.6–27.3°C.

additional.name: FishKeeper
additional.notes: Third-source cross-check: temperature 23–27°C; adult size 11 cm; SG 1.020–1.025; pH 8.1–8.4. Provided breeding detail (larvae require rotifers as first food). Used in temperature and size averaging.

fish.breedingNotes: Has been documented spawning in captivity with male guarding behavior; larvae require live rotifers as first food, making successful rearing challenging.
